**Seoul**, officially **Seoul Special Metropolitan City**, represents South Korea's capital and most populous urban center, alongside being the largest city across the entire Korean peninsula. The wider Seoul Metropolitan Area, encompassing Seoul along with Gyeonggi Province and Incheon, emerged as the world's "sixth largest metropolitan economy" in 2022, positioned after New York, Tokyo, Los Angeles, Paris, and London, while accommodating more than half of South Korea's total population. Though Seoul's population previously exceeded 10 million residents, it has experienced gradual decline since 2014, settling at approximately 9.6 million people as of 2024.

The city's documented history extends back to 18 BC when Baekje, representing one of the Three Kingdoms of Korea, established the settlement. Throughout the Joseon dynasty period, Seoul gained formal designation as the nation's capital, surrounded by fortification walls. Early twentieth-century Japanese occupation brought temporary renaming to "Keijo," though the city underwent significant wartime devastation during the Korean conflict.

An administrative transition has gradually shifted toward Sejong City, a planned capital that commenced operations in 2012 and sits approximately 121 kilometers southward. As of 2019, Sejong accommodates 10 of South Korea's 18 government ministries.
